text: Photo-controlled, Ru-based metallopolymer adhesive for reconfigurable hydrogel structures with tunable functionalities Responsive hydrogels with switchable shape changing functionalities are highly desirable for soft robotics, 4D printing and biomedical fields. Although considerable efforts have been devoted to design hydrogel structures for on-demand reconfiguration or multi-functionalities, there is still a long way to go to achieve heterogeneous hydrogel structures that can be tailored for versatile functions due to the permanent nature of covalent crosslinks. Here, we report the design and synthesis of photo-controlled Ru-based metallopolymer adhesive to "glue" and connect individual hydrogel components in a reversible manner. The adhesive is mediated by Ru-thioether coordination bonds and inter-polymer chain entanglements at the interface of the metallopolymer and hydrogel, enabling strong bonding under a wide range of conditions (such as thermal and pH changes) that are typically required to achieve actuation of the integrated hydrogel structures). After light induced dissociation of the coordination bonds for selective disassembling of the hydrogel structures, heating is employed to re-configure the disassembled structures into desired functionalities. Using this concept, multi-shape transformers and soft modular robot have been successfully fabricated and demonstrated to guide the movement through the labyrinth with precise morphological transitions. Overall, our study of using stimuli-responsive adhesives to modulate the self-assembly and re-configuration of individual hydrogel components provides a versatile strategy to fabricate complex smart materials with diverse and tailored functions.
